text.heroSub: A website dedicated to one of the most prominent movements in United States history.
text.whoTitle: If you want to remember me, organize.
text.whoBody: Los Chicanos, or 'The Chicanos', refers to people from Mexican descent, born on U.S. soil. In the midst of a predominantly caucasian society, Chicanos used a previously derogatory slur to empower themselves. Chicanos often advocated for better working conditions, improved healthcare, and increased pay for their labor. The Chicano Movement transformed into a multitude of organizations such as the United Farm Workers, MAYO, EICC, UMAS, all associated with different issues. Below are statistics from one of the most prominent of these organizations, the UFW, which is still highly relevant today.
text.causeIntro: The foundational document calling for the self-determination of the Chicano race, serving as a philosophical blueprint for the rise of the Chicano Movement.
text.flashIntro: Introduce the major events of the movement.
text.legacyText: The rise of the Chicano Movement in the 1960s/70s has empowered many members of the Hispanic community, encouraging those previously discriminated to take a stand, become active in politics, and make a difference nationally. Leaders such as Chavez and Huerta remain as an inspiration to those fighting for their own rights and identity.
text.footerNote: A student research project. Every quotation, image, and fact is documented in our Annotated Bibliography (MLA).

New contracts for higher wages, safer working conditions, and better healthcare plans.

goals.title: Unity
goals.text: The Chicano population must be unified, whether it be farmers, the upper or middle class, or the unemployed. Strength lies within continued commitment to liberating the race.

goals.title: Economic Control
goals.text: To secure economic prosperity amongst all Chicanos, exploiters must be removed and controlled. To succeed means ignoring materialism, embracing humanism, and providing an equal distribution of resources to improve the welfare of all of the community.

goals.title: Education
goals.text: All education must be relevant to Chicano history, culture, and preserving language. The power of the community starts with keeping it taught.

goals.title: Restitution
goals.text: For the discrimination, economic slavery, denial of rights, and utmost exploitation, Chicanos as a people must receive reparations through the establishment of institutions that support the development of Chicano citizens.

goals.title: Political Representation
goals.text: In order to continue supporting Los Chicanos, political representation must be secured through utmost unification. Local organizations and movements must combine to form one giant family. That way, the struggles of everyday citizens in the community will not be tied to those most fueled by money and power.

“A nation autonomously free, culturally, socially, economically and politically will make its own decisions on the usage of our lands, the taxation of our goods, the utilization of our bodies for war, the determination of justice (reward and punishment), and the profit of our sweat. EL PLAN DE AZTLAN IS THE PLAN OF LIBERATION” (Urista and Gonzales)! — El plan espiritual de Aztlan, 1969

leaders.name: Cesar Chavez
leaders.bio: Cesar Chavez was one of the main leaders of the Chicano movement and was the president of the NFWA until his death in 1993. His goal was to unionize farm workers and help give Mexican Americans the civil rights that they deserved by using nonviolent tactics. Throughout the movement, he organized strikes, protested, and helped the farm laborers register to vote. In fact, a research guide from the Library of Congress mentions how on February 14th, 1968, “César Chávez begins to fast in protest of farmworker exploitations” (Library of Congress).
leaders.quote: “Farm workers are not agricultural implements; they are not beasts of burden to be used and discarded.”

leaders.name: Dolores Huerta
leaders.bio: Dolores Huerta was a key civil rights activist and feminist during the Chicano movement. She broke down gender barriers and impacted many young women. Inspired by her mother Alicia, Huerta showed kindness and compassion to others, and she worked hard to fight for justice for Mexican Americans and women. Alongside Chavez, Huerta often protested against poor labor conditions and economic inequality, but she mainly challenged gender discrimination during the Chicano movement and labor movements.

leaders.name: Rosalio Muñoz
leaders.bio: Rosalio Muñoz was a well-known protestor and activist during the Chicano movement. With his organization (Chicano Moratorium), he tried to protest and get other people to protest against the draft in the Vietnam War, being anti-war. His protests had a lot of people and were often interrupted by violent police actions and gunfights, leading to the deaths of notable people like Ruben Salazar. Because of his protests, FBI undercover agents monitored him for terrorist activities and forcibly removed him in 1970, but he returned soon after to run the Moratorium again (Kratz).
